title: Noether's theorem: every continuous symmetry is a conservation law
intro: In autumn 1915, days after Einstein finished general relativity, David Hilbert wrote him with a worry: the new field equations seemed to lack an honest local conservation law for energy. The divergence of the stress-energy tensor (the object packaging energy and momentum densities of matter into a single covariant tensor) vanished in a covariant sense only, not as an integral conservation equation, and a coordinate change could shuffle energy around. Hilbert and Felix Klein passed the puzzle to Emmy Noether, the Erlangen-trained algebraist they had brought to Göttingen but could not pay because the philosophy faculty objected to a woman lecturing. Her 1918 paper turned the question into a theorem so general that it both explained the puzzle and dissolved it: every continuous symmetry of the action carries a conserved current, and general relativity belongs to a particular family of theories whose missing conservation law is exactly what one should expect.

blocks.text: In autumn 1915, days after Einstein finished general relativity, David Hilbert wrote him with a worry. The new theory had no honest local conservation law for energy in the way Newton's mechanics had one. In Newton's framework, conservation meant the rate of change of the energy inside any volume equaled minus the energy flux through its boundary; the books balanced everywhere, every instant. In GR that integral was not well defined as written. The volume measure depended on the metric (the dynamical field that encodes the geometry of spacetime), and a coordinate change could shuffle energy around. The covariant divergence of the stress-energy tensor, ∇_μ T^μν = 0, vanished by construction, but it was not the same statement as Newton's bookkeeping.

Hilbert and Felix Klein (the Göttingen geometer who had brought the German mathematical community to its post-Riemann zenith) passed the puzzle to Emmy Noether, an algebraist they had recruited from Erlangen in 1915 to work on invariant theory. The philosophy faculty had refused her habilitation, the formal qualification required to lecture at a German university, on the grounds that the regulations forbade women from teaching. Hilbert listed her courses under his own name, reportedly telling the faculty meeting, "I do not see that the sex of the candidate is an argument against her admission as a Privatdozent. After all, we are a university, not a bath house." She lectured under his title for four years.

Her 1918 paper in the Göttingen Nachrichten contained two theorems. The first: every continuous symmetry of the action yields an explicit conserved quantity, with a closed-form recipe for what is conserved. The second: every local symmetry (one whose parameter can vary smoothly from point to point, like the diffeomorphism invariance of general relativity) yields an off-shell identity, a constraint among the equations of motion themselves, rather than a new conservation law. Hilbert's puzzle was an instance of the second theorem. Einstein wrote Hilbert a few months later, "I am impressed that such things can be understood in so general a way." The 1918 paper was, and is, the deepest single result anyone has written down about the structure of classical mechanics.

A continuous symmetry of the action is a one-parameter family of transformations that nudges the trajectory by an infinitesimal amount and leaves the action S = ∫ L dt unchanged. Write the transformation as q(t) → q(t) + ε X(q, t) + O(ε²), where ε is the small parameter and X is the symmetry generator: a vector field on configuration space that tells you in which direction to shift each coordinate q_i. For a translation of x, X = (1, 0, 0). For a rotation in the x-y plane, X = (−y, x, 0). For time translation, X turns out to be different, and we will handle that case separately in the next section.
Noether's first theorem says that for any such symmetry, there is a conserved quantity Q whose form depends only on the Lagrangian L and the generator X.

blocks.tex: Q \;=\; \sum_i \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\, X_i \;=\; \text{const along the trajectory}
blocks.caption: L is the Lagrangian. q_i is the i-th generalized coordinate. q̇_i is its time derivative, the i-th generalized velocity. ∂L/∂q̇_i is the conjugate momentum p_i in the i-th coordinate, ordinary linear momentum for a Cartesian coordinate and angular momentum for an angular one. X_i is the i-th component of the symmetry generator, telling you in which direction to shift q_i under the transformation. The conserved quantity Q is the dot product of the conjugate momenta with the generator, evaluated along the trajectory.

blocks.text: Read the formula on its simplest case. For a pure translation along the j-th coordinate, X_i = δ_ij (one in the j-th slot, zero elsewhere), and Q reduces to the conjugate momentum p_j alone. So Noether's theorem says: if the Lagrangian is invariant under shifts in q_j, then p_j is conserved. That is exactly the cyclic-coordinate rule from the Lagrangian mechanics lesson. Noether's contribution was to generalize the rule to symmetries that mix coordinates with each other (rotations rotate x into y) and to symmetries that change L by a total time derivative (Galilean boosts), where a small correction term is needed. The recipe became the universal way to turn any continuous symmetry into a conserved charge.

quiz.question: A particle moves in two dimensions in a central potential V(r), where r = √(x² + y²), with Lagrangian L = (1/2)m(ẋ² + ẏ²) − V(r). Apply Noether's theorem with the rotation generator X = (−y, x). What conserved quantity does the formula Q = (∂L/∂ẋ) X_x + (∂L/∂ẏ) X_y give you?
options: Angular momentum L_z = m(x ẏ − y ẋ) is conserved. The rotation x → x − ε y, y → y + ε x leaves both the kinetic energy ẋ² + ẏ² and r² = x² + y² invariant, so the Lagrangian is unchanged, and Noether's formula gives m ẋ (−y) + m ẏ (x) = m(x ẏ − y ẋ).
Total energy E = T + V is conserved, because rotations are a symmetry of every central-force problem.
Linear momentum p = (m ẋ, m ẏ) is conserved, because rotations preserve the magnitude of the velocity.
Nothing is conserved, because rotations also rotate the velocity, and that breaks the symmetry of the kinetic energy.
quiz.explanation: The rotation transforms positions and velocities together, and the quadratic invariants ẋ² + ẏ² and x² + y² are preserved exactly under it. So the Lagrangian is invariant and Noether's formula gives Q = m(x ẏ − y ẋ) = L_z. The most tempting wrong answer is option 2: it confuses which symmetry gives which conservation law. Time translation gives energy; rotation gives angular momentum. Conflating them is how the entire conservation-law architecture comes apart. Option 3 is wrong because linear momentum is conserved only when the Lagrangian is translation-invariant, which a central potential V(r) is not. Option 4 misreads the calculation: velocities transform along with positions under a rotation, and the magnitude squared is unchanged.

blocks.text: Time translation, t → t + ε, is the symmetry that is most awkward to fit into the Noether formula directly. The transformation does not shift coordinates; it shifts the parameter time itself. The cleanest way to derive the conserved quantity in this case is not to plug into the general formula but to differentiate L along the trajectory and use the Euler-Lagrange equation as a chain-rule identity. Suppose L depends on q and q̇ but not explicitly on t, that is, ∂L/∂t = 0.

blocks.caption: L is the Lagrangian. q_i is the i-th coordinate, q̇_i its time derivative, q̈_i its acceleration. ∂L/∂q_i and ∂L/∂q̇_i are partial derivatives holding everything else fixed. ∂L/∂t is the explicit time derivative, the t-dependence the Lagrangian wears on its sleeve. The identity says: the total rate of change of L along the trajectory has three contributions, how L varies with the coordinates, with their velocities, and directly with t.

blocks.text: Use the Euler-Lagrange equation ∂L/∂q_i = d/dt (∂L/∂q̇_i) to rewrite the first term. The first two terms then combine into the time derivative of a product: q̇_i d/dt(∂L/∂q̇_i) + (∂L/∂q̇_i) q̈_i = d/dt[q̇_i (∂L/∂q̇_i)]. Move L on the left across, and you have a single identity.

blocks.caption: The bracketed expression is the energy function H = Σ_i q̇_i (∂L/∂q̇_i) − L. The identity says: the rate of change of H along a trajectory equals the negative of the Lagrangian's explicit time dependence. When ∂L/∂t = 0 (the Lagrangian carries no explicit t), dH/dt = 0 and H is conserved along the trajectory. For an ordinary L = T − V with kinetic energy T quadratic in the velocities, H comes out to T + V, the total mechanical energy.

blocks.text: So time-translation invariance, the single condition ∂L/∂t = 0, hands you energy conservation as a corollary. The viz below makes this visible. It simulates a 2D particle in a potential V(x, y, t), with a toggle for four choices of V. For each choice, it watches the four candidate Noether charges (E, p_x, p_y, L_z) and shows which stay flat over time and which drift. Free space (V = 0) keeps all four flat. A 1D harmonic V = ½ x² breaks x-translation and rotation but keeps y-translation and time-invariance, so only E and p_y stay flat. A central well V = ½(x² + y²) keeps rotation and time but breaks both translations: E and L_z stay flat, p_x and p_y drift. Drive the spring in time, V = ½(1 + 0.3 sin t)(x² + y²), and only L_z survives. Each surviving charge is the Noether current of the surviving symmetry.

blocks.caption: A 2D particle in V(x, y, t), with four toggle choices for the potential. The four readouts E, p_x, p_y, L_z each track the candidate Noether charge of one spacetime symmetry: time translation, x-translation, y-translation, z-rotation. Charges whose symmetry survives the potential stay flat; charges whose symmetry is broken drift. Restart to reset the particle at (x, y) = (1, 0) with velocity (0, 1). The whole quartet of conservation laws is just a list of which Noether generators commute with whatever V you typed in.

blocks.text: The most elementary application of the first theorem is to translations in space. A free particle in three dimensions has L = (1/2)m|v|², which is invariant under x_j → x_j + ε for any j and any constant ε. The generator of the translation along x_j is X_i = δ_ij. Noether's formula gives Q = ∂L/∂ẋ_j = m ẋ_j = p_j, and the equation of motion d/dt(m ẋ_j) = 0 was already telling us p_j is conserved. Three independent translation directions give three conserved momentum components. This is what physicists mean by the homogeneity of empty space: the laws of motion are the same here as there.

blocks.caption: L is the Lagrangian. x_j is the j-th Cartesian coordinate, ẋ_j its velocity. ∂L/∂x_j = 0 is the statement that the Lagrangian does not depend on x_j, only on its derivative. p_j is the conjugate momentum to x_j, defined as ∂L/∂ẋ_j (which equals m ẋ_j for a kinetic-energy term T = ½ m |v|²). The implication says: if the j-th coordinate is cyclic, its conjugate momentum is conserved.

blocks.text: Add a potential V(x) that depends on x but not on y or z. Translation invariance now survives only along the two axes the potential ignores: ∂L/∂y = 0 and ∂L/∂z = 0, so p_y and p_z stay conserved. The x-translation symmetry is broken, and p_x is not. The pattern is exact: symmetries that survive give conservation laws that survive, one for one.

Rotations work the same way. For a particle in a central potential V(r) with r = √(x² + y² + z²), the Lagrangian depends only on the rotational invariants r and v² = |v|². A rotation around the z-axis is x → x cos ε − y sin ε, y → x sin ε + y cos ε. The generator at ε = 0 is X = (−y, x, 0). Noether's formula gives Q = m ẋ (−y) + m ẏ (x) = m (x ẏ − y ẋ), the z-component of angular momentum. In a spherically symmetric potential all three rotation axes are symmetries, and all three angular-momentum components are conserved simultaneously.

blocks.tex: L_z \;=\; m\,(x\,\dot{y} - y\,\dot{x}) \;=\; \text{const when } L \text{ is invariant under rotation around the } z \text{-axis}
blocks.caption: L_z is the z-component of angular momentum, the conserved Noether charge of rotation symmetry around the z-axis. m is the particle's mass. x and y are Cartesian coordinates in the plane perpendicular to z, and ẋ and ẏ are their time derivatives. The combination x ẏ − y ẋ is the signed area swept per unit time by the position vector projected into the x-y plane, scaled by mass.

quiz.question: A particle moves in three dimensions in the potential V(x, y, z) = (1/2) k (x² + y²), a 2D harmonic well stacked uniformly along z. Which conserved quantities does Noether's theorem give you?
options: E, p_z, and L_z. The Lagrangian is invariant under time translation, z-translation, and rotation around the z-axis; Noether gives one conserved charge per surviving symmetry.
E and L_z only. The potential breaks all the translations because it depends on x and y, so p_z is not conserved either.
All four of E, p_x, p_y, p_z and all three components of angular momentum. The harmonic potential has full spherical symmetry.
Only E. Linear momentum requires a free particle and angular momentum requires a central potential, neither of which is the case here.
quiz.explanation: List the symmetries of L = ½ m |v|² − ½ k (x² + y²) one at a time. The Lagrangian does not depend on t, so time translation is a symmetry and E is conserved. It does not depend on z, so z-translation is a symmetry and p_z is conserved. It depends only on x² + y², so rotations around the z-axis leave it invariant and L_z is conserved. The other candidates (x-translation, y-translation, rotations around x or y) are broken by the potential's dependence on x and y, so the corresponding charges drift. Option 2 is the most tempting trap: it forgets that the potential ignores z entirely, so z-translation survives and p_z is conserved. Option 3 confuses a 2D harmonic well with the spherically symmetric V(r). Option 4 misunderstands the rule, treating linear and angular momentum conservation as requiring fundamentally different conditions, when in fact both come from the same Noether construction applied to different spacetime symmetries.

sections.heading: 5. The second theorem, and what Hilbert had actually asked

blocks.text: We have been using Noether's first theorem, on global symmetries. The 1918 paper has a second theorem, and that one is the part that answered Hilbert's original puzzle. The first theorem applies to symmetries whose parameter ε holds the same value at every spacetime point: translation by a fixed vector, rotation by a fixed angle, time translation by a fixed amount. The second theorem applies to local symmetries, transformations whose parameter ε(x) is allowed to vary smoothly from point to point. The diffeomorphism invariance of general relativity (the freedom to relabel events with any smooth coordinate change, four arbitrary functions of spacetime, and have the field equations still hold) is the canonical example.

A naive application of the first theorem to a local symmetry would give one conserved current per choice of ε at every point, infinitely many at once. That is too good to be true, and the second theorem says what actually happens. Local symmetries give off-shell identities, relations among the field equations themselves that hold whether or not the equations of motion are satisfied. They do not give new conservation laws.

For general relativity, the off-shell identity is the contracted Bianchi identity. Einstein's field equation reads G^μν = (8π G / c⁴) T^μν, where G^μν is the Einstein tensor (a particular combination of the Ricci tensor and the metric, built from the metric and its first and second derivatives) and T^μν is the stress-energy tensor of matter. Diffeomorphism invariance forces a single identity on the geometry side, and Einstein's equation then forces the same identity on the matter side.

blocks.caption: G^μν is the Einstein tensor, the combination of Ricci tensor and metric that sits on the left of Einstein's equation. T^μν is the stress-energy tensor of matter, packaging energy density, momentum density, and stress in a single covariant object. ∇_μ is the covariant divergence, the ordinary partial divergence corrected for the curvature of spacetime by Christoffel-symbol terms. The first identity says: the Einstein tensor has covariant divergence zero as a geometric fact, before any equation of motion is imposed. The implication says: combining the identity with Einstein's field equation forces the stress-energy tensor's covariant divergence to vanish too, which is the covariant version of energy-momentum conservation in general relativity.

blocks.text: The covariant law ∇_μ T^μν = 0 was the form of energy conservation in GR that bothered Hilbert. Newton's bookkeeping wanted ∂_μ T^μν = 0, an honest partial divergence that integrates over a region to give a total conserved energy on a fixed background. The two differ by Christoffel-symbol terms, and you cannot trade one for the other in a theory whose metric is itself dynamical, because the volume element that defines the integral depends on the metric. The integral conservation law Hilbert wanted is forbidden by the local symmetry of the theory. The covariant version is the strongest statement diffeomorphism invariance allows, and it is forced by Noether's second theorem. The 1918 paper closed an eighteen-month-old worry by showing it was a precise question with a precise answer: this is the only kind of energy conservation a diffeomorphism-invariant theory can have, and that is not a defect of general relativity but a structural consequence of the symmetry that defines it.

finalQuiz.question: Which one-line statement most precisely captures what Noether's first theorem says, in a way that distinguishes its content from the cyclic-coordinate rule already in Lagrange's 1788 'Mécanique analytique'?
options: Every continuous symmetry of the action carries with it a conserved current, where 'symmetry' means any one-parameter transformation that leaves the action invariant (up to a boundary term), not just a shift in a single coordinate, and 'conserved' means the current is constant in time along solutions of the equations of motion.
Every conserved quantity in physics corresponds to a continuous symmetry of the Lagrangian.
Cyclic coordinates have conserved conjugate momenta, and Noether reformulated this rule in a more elegant notation.
The total energy is conserved whenever the Lagrangian has no explicit time dependence.
finalQuiz.explanation: Lagrange's cyclic-coordinate rule was the special case where the symmetry is a pure shift in a single coordinate. Noether generalized this in two crucial ways. First, she allowed the symmetry transformation to mix coordinates with each other (rotations rotate x into y), to mix coordinates with time (Galilean boosts), and to change the Lagrangian by a total derivative (with a small correction in the conserved charge formula). Second, she replaced 'the Lagrangian does not depend on this coordinate' with 'the action is invariant under this continuous transformation,' a much broader condition that subsumed gauge symmetry and time translation under the same umbrella. Option 2 reverses the implication: Noether's theorem runs from symmetry to conservation, not the other way (the converse is a separate result and is more delicate to state). Option 3 misses the new content (rotations, boosts, gauge symmetries, time translation). Option 4 names one application without naming the structure that makes it a special case of something larger.
